coredata.dc:title: Bismuth oxide/nitrogen-doped carbon dots hollow and porous hierarchitectures for high-performance asymmetric supercapacitors
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2019 The Society of Powder Technology Japan. Published by Elsevier B.V. and The Society of Powder Technology Japan. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Society of Powder Technology Japan. Published by Elsevier B.V. and The Society of Powder Technology Japan.
coredata.dc:creator: Ji, Zhenyuan
Dai, Wenyao
Zhang, Shuailong
Wang, Guofan
Shen, Xiaoping
Liu, Kai
Zhu, Guoxing
Kong, Lirong
Zhu, Jun
coredata.dc:description: The development of high-performance anode materials is critical for next-generation asymmetric supercapacitor devices. Herein, an advanced anode of bismuth oxide/nitrogen-doped carbon dots (Bi2O3/NCDs) hollow and porous hierarchitecture was successfully developed by a facile two-step strategy. It is revealed that the presence of NCDs can not only induce the assembly of Bi2O3 porous nanosheets to hollow microspheres, but also remarkably improve the specific capacitances of them. Attributing to the intriguing properties of NCDs and the unique structure of Bi2O3/NCDs composites, the Bi2O3/NCDs hierarchitectures deliver an enhanced specific capacitance of approximately 1046 F g−1 at a current density of 1 A g−1. In particularly, an asymmetric supercapacitor was constructed by using the Bi2O3/NCDs hierarchical microspheres as anode and Ni(OH)2/NCDs nanosheets as cathode, which exhibits an ultrahigh energy density of about 79.9 Wh kg−1 at 770.9 W kg−1. These results indicate that the Bi2O3/NCDs hollow and porous hierarchitectures hold great potential as novel anode materials for energy storage.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Bismuth oxide
Hierarchitectures
Asymmetric supercapacitors
Energy storage

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) XRD patterns of Bi2O3 and Bi2O3/NCDs composites; (b) Raman spectra of Bi2O3/NCDs composites.
SEM images of (a) Bi2O3 and (b–d) Bi2O3/NCDs-2 hierarchitectures.
(a–e) SEM mapping images and (f) pore size distribution of the Bi2O3/NCDs-2 hierarchitectures.
(a) Bi 4f, (b) O 1s, (c) C 1s, and (d) N 1s spectra of the Bi2O3/NCDs-2 hierarchitectures.

(a) Cyclic voltammetry curves and (b) charge-discharge profiles of the Bi2O3/NCDs-2 hierarchitectures; (c) Charge-discharge profiles of Bi2O3 and Bi2O3/NCDs at 1 A g−1; (d) The specific capacitances of Bi2O3 and Bi2O3/NCDs at various current densities.

(a) Cyclic voltammetry curves, (b) Charge-discharge profiles and (c) Ragone plot of the Ni(OH)2/NCDs//Bi2O3/NCDs-2 asymmetric supercapacitor; (d) Cycling stability of the asymmetric supercapacitor at 2 A g−1.

head.ce:title: Bismuth oxide/nitrogen-doped carbon dots hollow and porous hierarchitectures for high-performance asymmetric supercapacitors

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang 212013, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang 212013, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The development of high-performance anode materials is critical for next-generation asymmetric supercapacitor devices. Herein, an advanced anode of bismuth oxide/nitrogen-doped carbon dots (Bi2O3/NCDs) hollow and porous hierarchitecture was successfully developed by a facile two-step strategy. It is revealed that the presence of NCDs can not only induce the assembly of Bi2O3 porous nanosheets to hollow microspheres, but also remarkably improve the specific capacitances of them. Attributing to the intriguing properties of NCDs and the unique structure of Bi2O3/NCDs composites, the Bi2O3/NCDs hierarchitectures deliver an enhanced specific capacitance of approximately 1046 F g−1 at a current density of 1 A g−1. In particularly, an asymmetric supercapacitor was constructed by using the Bi2O3/NCDs hierarchical microspheres as anode and Ni(OH)2/NCDs nanosheets as cathode, which exhibits an ultrahigh energy density of about 79.9 Wh kg−1 at 770.9 W kg−1. These results indicate that the Bi2O3/NCDs hollow and porous hierarchitectures hold great potential as novel anode materials for energy storage.

ce:section.ce:para: Recently, supercapacitors have elicited extensive attention in both scientific communities and industry because of their ideal power density and splendid cycling stability [1]. Supercapacitors have been used in transportation, wearable electronics, mobile electrical systems and so on [2,3]. Despite this, the extensive application of supercapacitors has been restricted due to their relative low energy densities [4,5]. Consequently, plentiful research efforts have been devoted to ameliorate the energy densities of supercapacitors without reducing their power densities. It is worth noting that constructing asymmetric supercapacitors is considered as an effective strategy to address the above challenge, which can take full advantage of the electrochemical characteristics of both cathode and anode materials [6]. Up to now, rapid progress has been made to develop high electrochemical properties cathodes, but only a few researches have been focused on the construction of advanced anodes. The commonly available anodes for asymmetric supercapacitors are carbonaceous materials on account of their remarkable electroconductibility and outstanding structural stability [7–9]. Nevertheless, these carbonaceous materials have the drawback of a poor specific capacitance, which greatly astrict the development of asymmetric supercapacitors. Very recently, as new class of anode materials for supercapacitors, metal oxides have elicited increasing interest due to their enhanced specific capacitances than carbonaceous materials [10–12]. Among them, bismuth oxide (Bi2O3) is considered as a potential candidate for asymmetric supercapacitors. The theoretical specific capacitance of Bi2O3 can reach up to 1370 F g−1 [13,14]. However, the actual capacitance of Bi2O3 is still restricted by the poor electrical conductivity [15]. On the other hand, the capacitance of Bi2O3 is directly related to its morphology and structure. Therefore, the development of high capacitive performance Bi2O3 anode materials with specific morphologies is highly desirable.

Carbon dots, zero-dimensional carbon nanoparticles, possess brilliant physical and chemical properties, such as high surface area, extraordinary surface grafting ability, and outstanding electron transfer/reservoir performance [16,17]. Due to the significant edge effects, carbon dots can be readily doped by other elements (such as N and S), which render them high concentration of active sites [18]. In addition, carbon dots exhibit strong hydrophilicity, which can enlarge the contact area between the electrodes and the electrolyte [19]. All the above merits enable carbon dots fascinating for energy conversion and storage [20]. However, as far as we know, the preparation and electrochemical characteristics of nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) modified Bi2O3 for asymmetric supercapacitors have rarely been reported.

On the other hand, Ni(OH)2 has been extensively studied as a cathode material for asymmetric supercapacitors due to its low cost and large theoretical specific capacitance [21,22]. Very recently, we have demonstrated a facile hydrothermal approach to synthesize Ni(OH)2/NCDs composites, which exhibit enhanced electrochemical characteristics than pristine Ni(OH)2 [23]. Inspired by it, in the present work, we demonstrate a two-step route for the preparation of hollow and porous Bi2O3/NCDs hierarchical microspheres. Compared to bare Bi2O3 nanosheets, the Bi2O3/NCDs hierarchitectures deliver an enhanced specific capacitance of 1046 F g−1 at 1 A g−1. More importantly, an asymmetric supercapacitor constructed by Bi2O3/NCDs and Ni(OH)2/NCDs achieves an ultrahigh energy density of 79.9 Wh kg−1 at 770.9 W kg−1.

Polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) dispersion was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. All other reagents were received from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. The NCDs powder was prepared according to our previous study [23]. The Bi2O3/NCDs hierarchitectures were synthesized as follows: Bi(NO3)3·9H2O (0.5 mmol) and NCDs powder (2.5, 5.0 and 7.5 mg) were dissolved in a mixed solution of acetic acid (5 mL) and ethanol (15 mL). Subsequently, the mixture was added into a 50 mL Teflon-lined autoclave. The autoclave was kept at 180 °C for 20 h. The Bi2O3/NCDs was gathered and washed with water and ethanol, followed by calcination at 400 °C for 3 h in Ar atmosphere with a ramp rate of 2 °C min−1. The composites were designated as Bi2O3/NCDs-1, Bi2O3/NCDs-2 and Bi2O3/NCDs-3 for feeding 2.5, 5.0 and 7.5 mg of NCDs powder, respectively. For comparation, bare Bi2O3 was also synthesized in the absence of NCDs powder.

The crystalline phase and structure of Bi2O3/NCDs composites were examined using X-ray diffraction (XRD, Smartlab-3kw) and Raman spectrometer (DXR, 532 nm laser light). The morphology and elemental mapping analyses were carried out on scanning electron microscopy (SEM, JSM-7800F). The zeta potentials of the products were performed on ZetaSizer Nano ZS90 analyzer (Malvern Instruments, U.K.). The composition of the composites was examined by inductively coupled plasma optical emission spectrometer (ICP-OES, Vista-MXP, Varian) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo ESCALAB 250XI). The pore size distribution of Bi2O3/NCDs composites was performed on a Micrometrics ASAP 2460 sorption instrument.

The electrochemical characteristics of Bi2O3/NCDs hierarchitectures were conducted on a CHI 760E analyzer in 3.0 M KOH aqueous solution at room temperature. For the three-electrode configuration, Bi2O3/NCDs, acetylene black and PTFE binder (8:1:1) were grounded in ethanol solvent. The obtained homogeneous mixture was directly pasted onto a Ni foam under a pressure of 10 Mpa, and then dried at 60 °C for 12 h. Each Ni foam contains approximately 2 mg of active material. A platinum foil and a Hg/HgO electrode were chosen as counter and reference electrode. The cyclic voltammetry curves were measured at sweep rates from 5 to 20 mV s−1 in a potential range of −1.0–0 V. The galvanostatic charge-discharge profiles were conducted at current densities of 1–15 A g−1. The asymmetric supercapacitor was assembled by choosing Bi2O3/NCDs hierarchitectures as anode and Ni(OH)2/NCDs composites as cathode. The Ni(OH)2/NCDs cathode was prepared according to our previous work [23]. The mass ratio of cathode to anode was optimized according to the charge balance [24]. The cyclic voltammetry curves were measured at various sweep rates in a potential range of 0–1.6 V. The galvanostatic charge-discharge profiles were conducted at different current densities.

The crystalline nature of Bi2O3 and Bi2O3/NCDs composites was firstly evaluated by XRD. As presented in Fig. 1 a, the Bi2O3 and Bi2O3/NCDs show similar XRD patterns. The diffraction peaks can be well assigned to tetragonal phase Bi2O3 (JCPDS No. 78-1793) and monoclinic phase Bi2O3 (JCPDS No. 71-2274). Due to the weak crystallinity of NCDs powder, the diffraction peaks of NCDs can hardly be observed in the XRD pattern of Bi2O3/NCDs [25]. To confirm the presence of NCDs in Bi2O3/NCDs, the composites were further investigated by Raman spectroscopy (Fig. 1b), which is a powerful technique for probing the structure of nanomaterials. In detail, the Raman bands at 309 and 445 cm−1 can be ascribed to the vibrational modes of Bi2O3 [26], while the other two broad bands at 1355 and 1581 cm−1 can be indexed to the characteristic D and G signals of NCDs. The intensities of D and G bands increase with the increase of NCDs content. In order to determine the mass ratios of Bi2O3 and NCDs in the composites, ICP-OES was carried out. The contents of Bi2O3 in Bi2O3/NCDs-1, Bi2O3/NCDs-2 and Bi2O3/NCDs-3 composites are determined to be about 98.8, 97.5 and 96.1 wt% according to ICP-OES analysis.

To confirm the chemical states of different elements in Bi2O3/NCDs composites, XPS characterization was performed (Fig. 4 and S1). In the high-resolution Bi 4f spectra of Bi2O3/NCDs composites, the two strong bands at 158.9 and 164.2 eV correspond to Bi 4f7/2 and Bi 4f5/2 of the Bi3+ in Bi2O3 [29,30]. The binding-energy peaks at 529.5 and 531.0 eV in O 1s spectrum (Fig. 4 b) attribute to the BiO bonds in Bi2O3 [14,31]. The C 1s spectrum in Fig. 4c can be fitted into three bands, which correspond to CC/CC (284.7 eV), CO (285.7 eV) and CN (288.4 eV), respectively [18,32]. The high-resolution N 1s peak is located at about 400 eV. This further suggests the successful doping of N element into the NCDs and the intimate integration of Bi2O3 and NCDs. The N 1s spectrum of Bi2O3/NCDs-1 composites can hardly be observed, which is due to the low content of NCDs in the composites.

To evaluate the electrochemical properties of the Bi2O3 and Bi2O3/NCDs, cyclic voltammetry and galvanostatic charge-discharge analyses were performed in 3.0 M KOH solution at room temperature. Fig. 5 a presents the cyclic voltammetry curves of Bi2O3/NCDs-2 hierarchitectures at various sweep rates. Obviously, well-defined redox bands can be observed in all the curves, resulting from the reversible faraday process between Bi2O3 and Bi [13,33]. The weak peaks at about −0.52 V attribute to the oxidation of the untransformed Bi during the electrochemical reactions [34]. With the increase of sweep rate, the current density of Bi2O3/NCDs-2 electrode increases gradually. In addition, the anodic peak of Bi2O3/NCDs-2 shifts to high potential and the cathodic peak shifts to low potential due to the relatively slow kinetics of the electrochemical reactions at higher sweep rates [35]. The charge-discharge profiles of the Bi2O3/NCDs-2 hierarchitectures at current densities of 1, 2, 5, 10 and 15 A g−1 are recorded in Fig. 5b. All curves show a quasi-symmetric shape, which indicates the high coulombic efficiency and excellent reversibility. The specific capacitances of Bi2O3/NCDs-2 hierarchitectures are determined by the formula:
(1)
C = I Δ t / ( m Δ V )
where C ( F g−1), I (A), △t (s), m (g) and △V (V) represent the specific capacitance, current, discharge time, mass of Bi2O3/NCDs-2 deposited on Ni foam, and the potential window, respectively. The Bi2O3/NCDs-2 hierarchitectures achieve specific capacitances of 1046, 964, 855, 787, 748 at 1, 2, 5, 10 and 15 A g−1, which are comparable to previously reported Bi2O3-based materials, such as Bi2O3/activated carbon (332.6 F g−1 at 1 A g−1) [36], Bi2O3/graphene (255 F g−1 at 1 A g−1) [37], and N-GNTs@OV-Bi2O3 (643 F g−1 at 1 A g−1) [15]. To illustrate the role of the NCDs in the electrochemical performance of Bi2O3, the charge-discharge profiles of bare Bi2O3 and other Bi2O3/NCDs composites were also recorded. As shown in Fig. 5c and 5d, the Bi2O3/NCDs composites deliver enhanced specific capacitances than that of bare Bi2O3. The specific capacitances of bare Bi2O3, Bi2O3/NCDs-1, Bi2O3/NCDs-2, and Bi2O3/NCDs-3 at 1 A g−1 are calculated to be 849, 896, 1046, and 917 F g−1, respectively. Moreover, the capacitance retention rates are 64.8%, 66.0%, 71.5%, and 76.9% at current densities from 1 to 15 A g−1. Thus, it is clear that the NCDs have a remarkable effect on the electrochemical characteristics of Bi2O3. The enhanced capacitive property of Bi2O3/NCDs can be attributed to the intriguing properties of the NCDs. However, excessive NCDs would hinder the contact between Bi2O3 and KOH, and the Bi2O3/NCDs-2 electrode exhibits the highest specific capacitances.

To evaluate the potential application of our prepared Bi2O3/NCDs hierarchitectures, an asymmetric supercapacitor was constructed. Fig. 6 a presents the cyclic voltammetry curves of Ni(OH)2/NCDs//Bi2O3/NCDs-2 under the potential range of 0–1.6 V. The redox peaks can be observed in these curves, which suggest that Ni(OH)2/NCDs//Bi2O3/NCDs-2 exhibits the contribution mainly from faradaic reaction. There was no significant change in the curves from 5 to 25 mV s−1. This suggests that the asymmetric supercapacitor possesses the ability of fast charge transfer. Similar to the cyclic voltammetry measurement, the nonlinear charge-discharge profiles of Ni(OH)2/NCDs//Bi2O3/NCDs-2 further demonstrate the faradaic characteristics (Fig. 6b). The energy densities and power densities of the asymmetric supercapacitor are

calculated by the equations [38]:
(2)
E = I ∫ V d t / M
(3)
P = E / t
where E (Wh kg−1), P (W kg−1), M (g) and ∫Vdt are the energy density, power density, total mass of both electrodes and integral area of the galvanostatic discharge curve, respectively. The Ni(OH)2/NCDs//Bi2O3/NCDs-2 delivers an ultrahigh energy density of 79.9 Wh kg−1 at 770.9 W kg−1, which is higher than those of previously reported asymmetric supercapacitors (Fig. 6c), such as CF@NiCo2O4//ESCNF@Bi2O3 [13], MnO2//Bi2O3 [39], NixCo3−xO4//AC [40], Ni(OH)2-CoQD//RGO [41], Ni(OH)2//HPC [42], and Ni(OH)2/NCDs//3DGE [23]. The cyclic stability of the asymmetric supercapacitor was further performed at 2 A g−1 (Fig. 6d), and about 83.5% of the initial capacity was retained after 1500 continuous cycles. In addition, the structural stability of the Bi2O3/NCDs-2 composites after the cycling test was also checked by SEM observation. As shown in Fig. S2, the morphology of the composites does not display noticeable changes after the electrochemical measurements, which confirms the high stability of our Bi2O3/NCDs composites.

In summary, Bi2O3/NCDs hollow and porous hierarchitectures were successfully prepared by a facile two-step route. The NCDs play a significant role on the microstructure of the bare Bi2O3, which can induce the assembly of Bi2O3 porous nanosheets to tightly packed hollow microspheres. Compared to bare Bi2O3 nanosheets, the novel Bi2O3/NCDs hierarchitectures exhibit an enhanced specific capacitance of 1046 F g−1 at 1 A g−1. Moreover, the fabricated Ni(OH)2/NCDs//Bi2O3/NCDs asymmetric supercapacitor delivers an ultrahigh energy density of 79.9 Wh kg−1 at 770.9 W kg−1. The improved electrochemical properties are credited to the intriguing properties of NCDs and the particular structures of Bi2O3/NCDs and Ni(OH)2/NCDs. Therefore, Bi2O3/NCDs hollow and porous hierarchitectures exhibit the bright prospects in energy storage and conversion systems.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 51602129 and 21875091), Natural Science Research of the Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions of China (No. 17KJB430007), and the Youth Talent Training Program of Jiangsu University.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apt.2019.11.018.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
